title: The list
testCase: Did you make a list with <ul> and <li>?
blocks.0.text: Here is the tool you were missing yesterday.
blocks.3.text: <ul> wraps the whole list and says "this is a list". <li> is one row of it — "list item". Each row gets its own <li>.
blocks.4.text: The bullets appear on their own. You did not put them there. Same thing again: you said what it is, the browser decided how it looks.
blocks.7.text: <ol> instead of <ul> — and the bullets become numbers. "Ordered list". You use it when the sequence means something: steps, a ranking, a recipe.
blocks.8.text: You do not choose by looks. You choose by meaning: does the order matter, or not? If you swap two items tomorrow and nothing changes — then it is a <ul>.
blocks.9.text: Turn the mess on the right into a real list.
